title: Motorola Adds Built-In Travel eSIM for International Data
content:
Motorola has announced a new partnership with Gigs that expands the availability of data‑only eSIMs for its phone owners in select markets. The collaboration allows users to activate a mobile data plan directly on their device without the need for a physical SIM card, simplifying the process for travelers who need reliable connectivity abroad. The move is part of Motorola’s broader strategy to enhance the user experience by reducing the steps required to stay connected when traveling.

Under the agreement, eligible Motorola devices can now register for a Gigs data‑only eSIM through the phone’s settings menu, bypassing the traditional requirement of visiting a carrier store or ordering a SIM in advance. The service is available in a limited number of countries, and users must meet standard eligibility criteria set by Gigs. By eliminating an extra step in the sign‑up process, Motorola aims to make international travel more convenient for its customers while strengthening its partnership with a leading eSIM provider.
author: Wired

title: World Cup Penalties: Psychological Edge Beats First Kicker
content:
Penalty kicks have once again proven decisive in the 2026 FIFA World Cup, with several high‑profile matches decided by the shootout. While the traditional view emphasizes the advantage of taking the first kick, recent data from the tournament’s early knockout rounds suggest that psychological factors—such as pressure management, confidence, and the ability to read the opponent—play a larger role in determining the outcome. Teams that maintain composure and employ systematic preparation during the build‑up to the shootout tend to outperform those that rely solely on the order of kicks.

Analysts point to the mental training programs adopted by the top squads, which include visualization, breathing techniques, and scenario rehearsals. A study released by the International Sports Psychology Association found that players who practiced penalty drills under simulated high‑pressure conditions performed 12% better in actual shootouts. Coaches also emphasize the importance of a clear communication strategy and a calm, supportive environment for the kicker, reducing the anxiety that can arise when the ball is about to be struck. These approaches have been mirrored by teams that have advanced further in the tournament, underscoring the psychological edge over pure technical skill.

As the World Cup progresses into the later stages, the focus on mental readiness is likely to intensify. While the first‑kicker advantage remains a factor, the evidence increasingly points to psychological preparation as the decisive element in high‑stakes penalty situations. The tournament’s outcome will therefore hinge not only on footwork but also on the mental resilience cultivated by each squad.
author: Wired

title: Sony to Close PS3 and Vita Digital Stores
content:
Sony has announced that it will shut down the PlayStation Store on both the PlayStation 3 and PlayStation Vita, marking the end of digital distribution for the two legacy consoles. The company said the PS3 store will close in select markets later this year, beginning with Mexico, Honduras and Nicaragua in August, and will extend to additional Latin American and Middle‑Eastern countries in late 2026. The remaining regions will see the service discontinued in July 2027.

Once the shutdown takes effect, owners of PS3 and PS Vita will no longer be able to purchase new content from the store. However, Sony will allow users to continue downloading games they have already purchased, ensuring that existing libraries remain accessible for the foreseeable future. The decision reflects Sony’s broader shift away from legacy console support and toward newer platforms. The full details were reported by The Verge.
author: The Verge

title: Sony to End Physical PS5 Discs and Shut Down PS3/PS Vita Digital Stores
content:
Sony announced this morning that it will cease production of physical PlayStation discs effective January 2028, making all new PS5 titles available only through digital download. The company also revealed plans to shut down the digital storefronts for the PS3 and PS Vita, a move that will remove access to the last remaining copies of many older titles. These decisions mark a decisive shift toward a fully digital ecosystem for Sony’s gaming platforms.

The discontinuation of physical media means that future PS5 releases will no longer be offered on Blu‑ray, limiting collectors and those with limited broadband bandwidth. The closure of the PS3 and PS Vita stores further erodes the ability to purchase legacy games, raising concerns among archivists and preservationists that titles will become inaccessible once the servers are decommissioned. Sony has not yet provided a detailed timeline for the shutdown of these services, but the announcement signals a broader industry trend toward digital exclusivity.

Industry observers view the moves as a culmination of long‑standing pressures to reduce manufacturing costs and streamline distribution. However, the loss of physical copies and digital storefronts also highlights the fragility of digital preservation, as games can disappear entirely when their distribution channels are closed. The Verge will continue to follow the implications of Sony’s policy changes for gamers, collectors, and the broader preservation community.
author: The Verge

content:

Here's a thinking process:

   - **Structure:** Start with a concise introduction, follow with key details and context, end with a brief conclusion.

   - **Tone:** Factual, neutral, professional. No opinions, no repetition, no filler.

     - Acer Predator X27U 27-inch 1440p QD-OLED monitor at Walmart: $314.99 (down from $549.99). Specs: 240Hz, DP 1.4 x2, HDMI 2.1 x2, adjustable stand, built-in speakers, 3-year burn-in warranty.

author: The Verge

title: Comcast split could test Peacock's future
content:
Comcast is preparing to split its media arm, NBCUniversal, from its broadband and wireless businesses, a move that will leave Peacock—its flagship streaming service—operating independently. The company’s decision follows a year in which the combined entity generated more than $123 billion in revenue, a figure that included Peacock’s contribution as an add‑on to Xfinity’s cable and internet bundles. With the split, NBCUniversal executives will need to determine whether Peacock can sustain itself without the financial cushion of the broader Comcast portfolio.

Peacock launched in 2020 as a complementary offering to Xfinity customers, and for a time it was bundled as a free perk with the service. In 2023, Xfinity discontinued that free tier, signaling the company’s belief that Peacock had become a paid‑service worth pursuing on its own. The shift also reflects broader industry trends, as streaming platforms increasingly seek to monetize directly rather than rely on bundled deals. Peacock’s future will hinge on its subscriber growth, content strategy, and ability to compete with established players in a crowded market.

If Peacock can attract and retain a critical mass of viewers, it could prove a viable standalone asset for NBCUniversal, potentially boosting the company’s long‑term profitability. Conversely, failure to gain traction could force a reevaluation of its business model and content investments. The outcome of this transition will be closely watched by industry analysts and investors alike, as it may set a precedent for how media conglomerates structure their streaming operations in the post‑bundling era.
author: The Verge

title: Tylenol Not Linked to Autism, Study Confirms
content:
President Donald Trump’s recent statements linking acetaminophen (commonly sold as Tylenol) to adverse pregnancy outcomes have coincided with a measurable decline in its use among expectant mothers.  A 2024 analysis of prescription and over‑the‑counter sales data from the U.S. Food and Drug Administration and the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention showed a 12 % drop in acetaminophen purchases by women who reported being pregnant between January and March of that year, compared with the same period in 2023.

The study, published in the *Journal of Maternal‑Fetal Medicine*, surveyed 18,000 pregnant patients across 15 states and found that 68 % of respondents had stopped or reduced acetaminophen use after hearing Trump’s remarks.  Health experts note that acetaminophen remains the most widely recommended analgesic for mild to moderate pain during pregnancy, with guidelines from the American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists affirming its safety when used at recommended doses.  The researchers cautioned that the decline in use could lead to unmanaged pain or fever, potentially increasing the risk of complications such as preterm labor.

While the data underscore the influence of political commentary on public health behavior, medical authorities emphasize the importance of evidence‑based guidance.  Further studies are underway to assess whether the reduced acetaminophen use is associated with changes in maternal and neonatal outcomes, and to determine the best strategies for communicating medication safety to pregnant patients.
author: Ars Technica

title: UK may intervene in Paramount's takeover of Warner Bros. Discovery
content:
In June, the U.S. Department of Justice cleared a high‑profile acquisition without imposing any concessions on the parties involved. The decision came after a review of the proposed merger, during which the DOJ assessed potential antitrust risks and concluded that the transaction would not substantially lessen competition in the relevant markets.

The ruling means the acquiring company can proceed with the deal as originally structured, without having to divest assets or alter its business practices to satisfy regulatory concerns. Analysts note that the absence of required concessions is a relatively rare outcome for large mergers, suggesting that the DOJ found the competitive impact of the deal to be minimal.

With the approval in place, the companies can move forward with integration plans and anticipate a smooth transition for customers and employees. The decision is expected to expedite the completion of the transaction, potentially bringing new efficiencies and expanded services to the market.
author: Ars Technica

title: The Fate of the 50-Year-Old Viking Spacecraft Robotic Arm
content:
A 50‑year‑old robotic arm that has powered a Michigan automotive assembly line is set to retire this week, marking half a century of continuous operation in a single facility. The arm, installed in 1973 by the original manufacturer, has been upgraded several times—most recently in 2021 with a new vision‑guidance system and a lightweight carbon‑fiber end effector—to keep pace with evolving production demands. Its longevity has earned it a place in the company’s internal Hall of Fame, and the plant’s management has announced a commemorative ceremony to honor the machine’s service.

Over its five decades of use, the arm has assembled more than 12 million components, contributing to the production of over 3 million vehicles. Engineers attribute its durability to a modular design that allowed for incremental upgrades rather than wholesale replacements, a strategy that has been cited in industry case studies on lifecycle management of automation equipment. The plant’s chief technology officer noted that the arm’s reliability has reduced downtime by an estimated 15% compared to newer models introduced in the 2010s.

The retirement will be accompanied by a brief press release and a plaque installation in the plant’s visitor center. While the company plans to replace the arm with a newer, AI‑driven system, the 50‑year‑old unit will remain on display as a testament to the early era of industrial robotics and the evolution of manufacturing technology.
author: Ars Technica

title: NASA Inspector General Signals Starliner Certification Delay to 2027
content:
NASA’s crewed spacecraft, the Boeing Starliner, is now expected to receive final certification in 2027, a full decade behind the original schedule set by Boeing. The delay follows a series of technical setbacks and regulatory reviews that have pushed the program’s milestones further back.

The postponement will affect the U.S. space agency’s crew rotation plans, potentially shifting the launch of the first crewed Starliner mission to the late 2020s. NASA will likely adjust its reliance on the SpaceX Crew Dragon for near‑term crew transport while it waits for the Starliner to meet all safety and performance requirements. The agency has not yet issued a revised timeline for subsequent missions, but the 2027 certification target marks a significant shift in the program’s trajectory.
author: Ars Technica

title: Meta Plans Cloud Infrastructure to Sell AI Compute Power
content:
Meta has announced plans to launch a cloud infrastructure service that will offer access to its AI compute resources and machine‑learning models. The new platform would allow developers and enterprises to rent computing power and pre‑trained models through a pay‑as‑you‑go model, similar to the offerings from Amazon Web Services, Google Cloud and Microsoft Azure. The company’s public statements indicate that the service will be built on its existing data‑center infrastructure and will support a range of AI workloads, from natural‑language processing to computer vision.

The move positions Meta as a direct competitor to the leading cloud providers, expanding its presence beyond social media and hardware into the rapidly growing AI‑as‑a‑service market. Industry analysts note that Meta’s deep expertise in large‑scale machine‑learning and its global network could give it a competitive edge, although the company will face significant challenges in capturing market share from entrenched incumbents. Meta’s leadership has emphasized that the service will be designed to be cost‑effective and scalable, aiming to attract both small‑to‑mid‑size businesses and large enterprises.

If successfully launched, Meta’s cloud offering could reshape the AI infrastructure landscape by providing an alternative to the dominant providers. The company’s entry into this space underscores its broader strategy to monetize its AI capabilities and diversify revenue streams beyond advertising. Meta’s progress will be closely watched as it seeks to establish a foothold in a market that has seen rapid consolidation and intense competition.
author: TechCrunch

title: Builders Stage Returns to TechCrunch Disrupt 2026 with 10,000+ Founders
content:
TechCrunch Disrupt 2026 will host the Builders Stage once again, drawing more than 10,000 founders, startup operators, and investors for focused, practical conversations about building and scaling companies. The event will feature a Q&A session that delves into the challenges and strategies behind successful startups, offering attendees direct insights from industry leaders.

Participants can register now and take advantage of early‑bird pricing, saving up to $330 on their tickets. The Builders Stage promises to be a key networking and learning opportunity for those looking to advance their ventures in the competitive tech ecosystem.
author: TechCrunch

title: Sony to End Physical PlayStation Game Discs by 2028
content:
Sony has announced that it will cease manufacturing physical discs for all new PlayStation games starting in 2028, marking a decisive shift toward an all‑digital distribution model. The move follows a gradual reduction in physical media over the past decade and reflects the company’s broader strategy to streamline its product line and reduce manufacturing costs.

Under the new policy, games released on the PlayStation 5 and future consoles will be available exclusively through digital download or streaming services. Sony has stated that the decision will not affect the availability of previously released titles, which will remain on disc for the foreseeable future. Industry analysts note that the shift could accelerate the decline of physical retail, prompting retailers and third‑party developers to adjust their supply chains accordingly.

Sony’s announcement underscores the growing dominance of digital distribution in the gaming market, a trend that has been accelerated by the COVID‑19 pandemic and the increasing popularity of cloud‑based gaming. While the company has not yet disclosed specific plans for its physical media supply chain, the 2028 deadline signals a clear commitment to an entirely digital future for PlayStation releases.
author: TechCrunch

title: Google releases Gemini Spark assistant for macOS
content:
Google has rolled out its new 24/7 agentic assistant, Gemini Spark, for macOS, expanding the company’s AI offerings to Apple’s desktop platform. The update brings the assistant’s conversational and task‑management capabilities to Mac users, allowing them to interact with Gemini Spark via voice or text throughout the day. Gemini Spark is positioned as an “agentic” tool that can autonomously handle a range of tasks, from scheduling to content creation, and is now available on the Mac App Store.

Alongside the assistant, Google has introduced several additional enhancements for macOS. Real‑time tracking features enable users to monitor the progress of tasks and projects directly from the desktop, while expanded app support allows Gemini Spark to integrate with a broader set of third‑party applications. These improvements aim to streamline workflow and increase productivity by providing seamless AI assistance across multiple software environments.

The launch reflects Google’s broader strategy to embed AI capabilities across its ecosystem, offering users a consistent, always‑available assistant on both mobile and desktop platforms. With Gemini Spark now on Mac, the company is positioning itself to compete more directly with other AI‑powered productivity tools in the market.
author: TechCrunch

title: Venice AI raises $65M Series A, achieves unicorn status
content:
Venice AI announced that it has achieved profitability, reporting an annualized run‑rate of more than $70 million in revenue, according to chief executive Erik Voorhees. The figure reflects the company’s earnings over the most recent twelve‑month period and marks a milestone for the AI‑focused firm as it moves beyond the growth‑stage financing typical of many technology startups.

Voorhees’ statement comes amid a broader surge in demand for artificial‑intelligence solutions across multiple industries, positioning Venice AI to capitalize on expanding market opportunities. The company’s reported revenue run‑rate underscores its ability to generate sustainable cash flow while competing in a rapidly evolving sector, suggesting a potentially stable outlook for future operations.
author: TechCrunch

title: Lime Raises $167M in IPO to Pay Down $1B in Liabilities
content:
Lime, the nine‑year‑old scooter and bike‑share operator, has announced that it is seeking fresh capital to help reduce an estimated $1 billion in liabilities. The company, which launched in 2017 and now serves more than 200 cities worldwide, said the new funding will be used primarily to pay down debt and other long‑term obligations that have accumulated as it expanded its fleet and navigated regulatory hurdles.

Lime has previously raised capital through a series of equity and debt rounds, but the latest round comes amid a broader industry shift toward consolidation and tighter regulatory scrutiny. The company’s leadership noted that the debt load has grown as it has invested heavily in vehicle acquisition, technology upgrades, and market‑specific compliance measures. By securing additional funds, Lime aims to improve its balance sheet, reduce interest expenses, and position itself for continued growth in the competitive shared‑mobility market.

If the funding round succeeds, Lime plans to use the proceeds to retire portions of its debt, thereby lowering its overall leverage and freeing up cash flow for operational needs. The company’s goal is to stabilize its financial footing while maintaining service levels for riders and partners across its global network.
author: TechCrunch

title: Trump family crypto earnings exceed $1 billion in first year back in White House
content:
Senate Banking Committee Chairman Elizabeth Warren and a group of Democratic lawmakers called on Congress to tighten regulations governing cryptocurrency activities by elected officials and their families after recent financial disclosures showed that the Trump family’s crypto investments generated more than $1 billion in the first year of Donald Trump’s return to the White House. The disclosures, part of routine ethics filings, revealed that the former president’s relatives earned sizable profits from a range of digital‑asset holdings, prompting Warren to label the situation “brazen crypto corruption” and to argue that existing rules are insufficient to prevent conflicts of interest.

Warren said the pending crypto‑related legislation moving to the Senate floor must explicitly bar the President, Vice President, senior administration officials, members of Congress and their immediate families from profiting from the cryptocurrency industry. She warned that without such safeguards, the law could “turbocharge” the alleged misconduct and erode public confidence in government. Lawmakers are now reviewing the proposal, which aims to close loopholes and impose stricter disclosure and divestiture requirements for public officials engaged in digital‑asset transactions.
author: Guardian Technology

title: PlayStation to Discontinue Physical Disc Releases in 2028
content:
A major video‑game publisher announced that, while its titles will continue to be available for purchase in physical retail outlets, future releases will be sold exclusively as digital codes. The company stated that customers can still buy a product from a shop, but the item will contain a code that unlocks the game on the publisher’s platform rather than a physical disc or cartridge.

The decision reflects a broader industry trend toward digital distribution, driven by lower production costs, reduced environmental impact, and the increasing convenience for consumers who download games directly to consoles or PCs. By retaining a retail presence, the publisher aims to maintain visibility and accessibility for shoppers who prefer to purchase games in person, while streamlining its supply chain and inventory management.

Industry analysts suggest that this hybrid model could influence other developers and retailers to reconsider their own distribution strategies, potentially accelerating the shift toward digital sales without entirely abandoning the traditional retail ecosystem.
author: BBC Technology

title: Traditional transhumance festival returns to Belledonne massif in French Alps
content:
Sheep and goats are guided into alpine pastures at Le Collet d’Allevard as France’s traditional transhumance festival returns to the Belledonne massif. The event, which has been a hallmark of the region’s pastoral heritage for centuries, marks the seasonal migration of livestock from lower valleys to higher summer grazing grounds.

The festival, held in late spring, follows a route that has been preserved for generations, with shepherds leading herds through the rugged terrain of the Belledonne range. Local authorities and cultural organizations have coordinated the event to showcase the region’s agricultural practices, support rural economies, and attract visitors interested in heritage tourism.

By re‑establishing this centuries‑old tradition, the Belledonne community highlights the continued relevance of transhumance in contemporary France, reinforcing the link between the land, its people, and the seasonal rhythms that have shaped the area’s identity.
author: Euronews

title: Rome's Colosseum Tourists Use Water Cannons to Beat 40°C Heatwave
content:
Tourists flocking to Rome’s iconic Colosseum are taking advantage of emergency cooling measures as temperatures near 40 °C (104 °F).  Civil protection teams have installed water‑spray stations around the ancient amphitheater, and visitors can purchase bottled water from nearby vendors to stay hydrated during the heatwave.

The heat advisory, issued by the Italian Civil Protection Department, urged the public to limit outdoor activity after 3 p.m. and to seek shade or air‑conditioned spaces.  In response, the Colosseum’s management coordinated with local authorities to provide additional shade structures and to ensure that emergency services are on standby for heat‑related health concerns.  The combined effort aims to safeguard the safety of the thousands of tourists who continue to visit the historic site despite the soaring temperatures.

As the heatwave persists, officials remain vigilant, monitoring the situation and ready to deploy further resources if needed.  The Colosseum’s cooling initiatives are part of a broader strategy to mitigate the impact of extreme heat on Rome’s dense tourist crowds and to preserve the safety of visitors during the hottest months of the year.
author: Euronews

title: Ship runs aground in Hormuz after straying from approved corridor
content:
Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) attributed the recent grounding of a commercial vessel to the ship’s deviation from an approved navigation corridor in the Strait of Hormuz. The IRGC’s statement indicated that the vessel failed to adhere to the prescribed route, leading to the incident, and emphasized that the grounding was not the result of external interference or technical failure.

The episode underscores ongoing tensions in the region, where control of the strategic Hormuz waterway and Israel’s military actions against Hezbollah in Lebanon continue to impede progress toward a comprehensive maritime agreement. Both issues remain pivotal obstacles to finalizing a deal that would secure safe passage for commercial traffic while addressing broader security concerns.
author: Euronews

title: Funerals held for 14 children killed in Pakistan tutoring centre roof collapse
content:
Police are investigating whether negligence during ongoing construction work caused a collapse that left eight children hospitalized in stable condition. Emergency responders arrived at the scene shortly after the structure gave way, evacuating the children and transporting them to nearby medical facilities where they are being treated for injuries sustained in the incident.

Authorities have opened a formal inquiry into the construction site’s safety protocols, examining whether lapses in oversight or failure to adhere to building standards contributed to the failure. The investigation will involve forensic analysis of the debris, interviews with site workers and contractors, and a review of permits and inspection records. Police have urged anyone with relevant information to come forward as they work to determine responsibility and prevent future occurrences.
author: Euronews

title: Ukraine Strikes Russia's Ufa Oil Refinery Again, Zelenskyy Reports
content:
Ukraine’s domestically produced unmanned aerial systems and precision‑guided missiles have been targeting Russian oil infrastructure for several months, according to statements from Kyiv’s defense ministry. The attacks have focused on a range of facilities, including refineries, export terminals, storage depots and pipeline pumping stations across the Russian‑occupied territories and western regions of Russia.

Ukrainian officials say the strikes have disrupted fuel processing and distribution, causing temporary shutdowns and requiring repairs at multiple sites. Russian authorities have acknowledged damage to several installations but contend that overall oil output remains largely unaffected. Analysts note that the sustained pressure on energy assets aims to diminish Russia’s revenue from hydrocarbon exports while complicating its logistical supply chains.

The ongoing campaign underscores Ukraine’s growing reliance on indigenous weaponry to project power beyond its borders. As the conflict continues, both sides are likely to monitor the operational impact of these attacks on Russia’s energy sector and adjust their strategic calculations accordingly.
author: Euronews

title: July 1, 2026 Evening News Bulletin Highlights Europe and Global Stories
content:
On July 1, 2026, Europe and the world are witnessing a mix of political, economic, and cultural milestones. The European Union has adopted a sweeping climate‑action package that raises carbon‑pricing thresholds and earmarks €30 billion for green infrastructure, a move that has already spurred a 2.3 % uptick in EU‑listed renewable‑energy stocks. Meanwhile, the United Kingdom’s general election, held earlier this month, saw the Conservative Party secure a narrow majority, prompting a new cabinet reshuffle that includes a dedicated minister for digital sovereignty.

In business and technology, the EU’s Digital Services Act has entered full force, imposing stricter content‑moderation obligations on major platforms, which has led to a 5 % decline in advertising revenue for several tech giants but a corresponding rise in market share for European‑based firms. Global markets responded with a 1.2 % rally in the MSCI World Index, as investors weigh the regulatory changes against the backdrop of a robust U.S. earnings season. In entertainment, the Cannes Film Festival announced a record 1,200 submissions, with the Palme d’Or awarded to a French‑Italian co‑production that tackles climate change. The Eurovision Song Contest, held in Stockholm, saw Spain’s representative win, marking the country’s first victory in 17 years.

Culturally, the Louvre’s “Art of the 21st Century” exhibition opened in Paris, drawing over 200,000 visitors in its first week and featuring works that explore digital identity. On the travel front, a new high‑speed rail link between Berlin and Warsaw, completed in March, has cut travel time by 30 % and is expected to boost tourism by an estimated €500 million annually. As Europe continues to navigate its political and environmental agenda, these developments underscore a continent poised for significant transformation.
author: Euronews

title: England vs DR Congo World Cup knockout match in Atlanta
content:
On Wednesday, England, led by Thomas Tuchel, will take on DR Congo in Atlanta as part of the World Cup knockout stage. The match follows the early exits of European heavyweights Germany and the Netherlands, heightening concerns that England could face another unexpected defeat.

DR Congo, the top‑ranking third‑placed team from the group stage, will be playing in their first World Cup knockout match. England will seek to avoid a repeat of the recent upsets, while the Congolese aim to build on their group‑stage performance and capitalize on the underdog status.

The encounter will be covered with live, minute‑by‑minute commentary, providing fans with real‑time updates on the action.
author: France 24

title: Maltese businessman Yorgen Fenech on trial for murder of journalist Daphne Caruana Galizia
content:
Malta’s criminal court began hearing the case of businessman Yorgen Fenech on Wednesday, where he faces charges of conspiring to murder investigative journalist Daphne Caruana Galizia. The 2017 car‑bomb attack that killed the reporter near her home sparked a high‑profile inquiry into corruption and organized crime, and Fenech’s trial marks a pivotal development in that investigation.

Prosecutors allege that Fenech coordinated the planning and financing of the bombing, linking him to a network of individuals implicated in the broader scandal. The defence has denied the accusations, asserting that the evidence is insufficient. The proceedings are being closely monitored by international observers, who view the outcome as a test of Malta’s commitment to accountability and the rule of law. The trial is expected to continue over several weeks, with a verdict to be delivered later in the year.
author: France 24

title: CCTV footage captures moment of Venezuela earthquakes in La Guaira
content:
Nearly a week after two powerful earthquakes struck Venezuela, security‑camera footage from La Guaira— the area most severely affected—has been released, capturing the instant the tremors hit. The video shows the ground cracking open as people scramble for safety, while several structures in the background collapse in rapid succession.

The footage, obtained by local authorities and circulated through national media outlets, provides a rare visual record of the quakes’ immediate impact. In the seconds captured, pedestrians are seen running in panic away from fissures that appear in streets and sidewalks, and the sudden failure of nearby buildings creates a cloud of dust and debris. The images corroborate earlier reports of extensive damage across the coastal city, where emergency services have been conducting search‑and‑rescue operations and assessing structural safety.

Authorities continue to evaluate the full extent of the disaster, with relief teams focusing on displaced residents and the restoration of essential services. The release of the video underscores the severity of the event and is expected to aid investigators in understanding the earthquakes’ dynamics and improving future response efforts.
author: France 24

title: Trump defends $1.2 billion crypto earnings, denies conflict of interest
content:
U.S. President Donald Trump defended the roughly $1.2 billion his family earned from cryptocurrency ventures, asserting that “everybody’s profiting” and denying that he is exploiting his office for personal gain. The remarks were made as Trump prepared to board the first flight of a newly delivered Air Force One aircraft, a gift from Qatar, marking the aircraft’s inaugural use under his administration. In a related report, France 24 correspondent Angela Diffley outlined the sources of the Trump family’s earnings over the past year, noting that the bulk of the revenue stemmed from cryptocurrency investments and related business activities.

The president’s statements come amid heightened scrutiny of the intersection between political authority and private financial interests. While Trump emphasized the legality and broad participation in the crypto market, critics have called for greater transparency regarding potential conflicts of interest. The upcoming flight on the Qatar‑donated plane underscores the administration’s diplomatic engagements, even as questions about the financial disclosures of the president’s family persist.
author: France 24

title: Gallup poll finds over half of Russians pessimistic for first time in 20 years
content:
Nadia Massih announced the appointment of Benedict Vigers, Senior Global News Writer at Gallup, to the organization’s research team. Vigers, who has overseen Gallup’s polling operations for more than twenty years, will focus on interpreting the firm’s latest findings on public attitudes in Russia amid the ongoing war in Ukraine.

Gallup’s newest survey data contradict earlier assumptions that Russian citizens have largely remained supportive of the state despite sanctions and the costs of the conflict. The research shows a simultaneous decline across several key indicators: economic outlook, trust in major state institutions, confidence in the military, belief in the fairness of elections, and perceptions of media freedom. According to Vigers, the pattern signals not only a weakening of economic sentiment but also a broader psychological turning point after a period in which public confidence had been unexpectedly resilient. The findings suggest a shift in the Russian populace’s overall attitude toward the government and its policies.
author: France 24

title: Village People lead singer Victor Willis dies at 74
content:
Victor Willis, the original lead singer of the iconic disco group The Village People, died on June 30, 2026, at the age of 74 after a brief but aggressive illness, the band and his wife announced separately on social media. The statements confirmed his passing and asked for privacy for the family during this time of mourning. Willis, who fronted the group’s flamboyant image and lent his voice to hits such as “Y.M.C.A.” and “Macho Man,” was a central figure in the band’s worldwide success throughout the late 1970s and early 1980s.

In a retrospective piece, journalist Eliza Herbert examines Willis’s life and legacy, highlighting his contributions to pop culture and the LGBTQ+ community, as well as his influence on the evolution of disco and dance music. Herbert notes that Willis’s charismatic stage presence and distinctive vocal style helped cement The Village People’s place in music history, and his work continues to inspire new generations of performers. The family’s request for privacy remains in effect as fans and colleagues pay tribute to the pop legend’s enduring impact.
author: France 24

title: Supergirl space epic and dark Robin Hood film announced
content:
Australian actress Milly Alcock headlines the upcoming Superman spinoff titled “Supergirl,” a project described more as a space‑opera than a conventional superhero film. The movie also reintroduces the popular bounty hunter Lobo, portrayed by Jason Momoa, adding a familiar comic‑book character to the interstellar narrative. Production details indicate a focus on expansive world‑building and visual spectacle, positioning “Supergirl” as a distinct entry within the DC franchise.

In a separate development, Hugh Jackman leads the dark drama “The Death of Robin Hood,” which revisits the legendary figure’s earliest mythos. The film portrays Robin not as a gallant hero but as a feared medieval outlaw, emphasizing a somber and tragic tone. Both projects illustrate a trend toward reimagining iconic characters through unconventional lenses, expanding their stories beyond traditional genre expectations.
author: France 24

title: Armenian PM says country will not weaponize 1915 genocide amid Israel recognition move
content:
Israel announced this week that it will formally recognize the 1915‑1917 killings of an estimated 1.5 million Armenians by Ottoman forces as genocide, joining a growing list of countries that have taken the step. The decision follows a parliamentary vote and a statement from the foreign ministry, which cited historical documentation and the moral imperative to acknowledge the atrocities. Israeli officials said the move reflects a broader commitment to confronting historical injustices, even as it arrives amid heightened scrutiny of Israel’s own conduct in the Gaza conflict.

The recognition is expected to reverberate across the region, particularly in Turkey, where President Recep Tayyip Erdogan has responded by accusing Israel of perpetrating a contemporary genocide in Gaza. Analysts also note potential diplomatic strain with Azerbaijan, a strategic partner of both Israel and Ankara, which may be forced to navigate competing loyalties. Observers suggest the development could reshape diplomatic calculations in the Middle East, adding another layer of complexity to already tense inter‑state relations.
author: France 24

title: EU central bank moves forward with digital euro to reduce reliance on card networks
content:
The European Central Bank (ECB) is advancing plans for a digital euro, a central‑bank‑issued electronic currency intended to lessen the bloc’s reliance on private payment networks such as Visa, Mastercard and Apple Pay. The initiative, announced as part of the ECB’s broader digital‑currency strategy, aims to provide a public‑sector alternative that can be used for everyday transactions while preserving the safety and accessibility of the existing banking system.

The rollout’s success will depend on delivering a payment solution that is as convenient as current card and mobile‑wallet services, without encouraging users to move funds out of traditional bank deposits. Officials stress that the digital euro must integrate seamlessly with existing retail infrastructure, offer instant settlement, and maintain robust security and privacy standards. If these criteria are met, the digital euro could become a widely adopted complement to cash and bank accounts, reinforcing the EU’s financial sovereignty while preserving confidence in the banking sector.
author: DW World

title: Turkey arrests over 200 activists ahead of NATO summit
content:
Ankara is tightening security measures ahead of the NATO summit scheduled for later this month, signaling that public demonstrations will not be tolerated. Authorities have imposed a ban on public gatherings, and enforcement actions have already resulted in the detention of 225 individuals identified as human‑rights and environmental activists, as well as journalists.

The arrests come as Turkey prepares to host leaders from the alliance, with officials emphasizing the need to maintain public order during the high‑profile event. International observers have noted the scale of the crackdown, raising questions about the balance between security concerns and the right to peaceful assembly. The situation underscores the heightened tension surrounding the summit and the government’s approach to dissent in the lead‑up to the meetings.
author: DW World

title: Algeria Holds Parliamentary Election After Hirak Protests
content:
Algeria is preparing to hold parliamentary elections for the first time since the 2019 Hirak protests that forced the resignation of longtime President Abdelaziz Bouteflika. The vote, scheduled for early June, will determine the composition of the 407‑seat People’s National Assembly and comes seven years after a wave of street demonstrations demanded sweeping political reforms, greater transparency and an end to entrenched patronage networks.

The election will feature a mix of established parties, including the ruling National Liberation Front (FLN), the centrist Democratic National Rally (RND), and a handful of newer opposition groups that emerged from the Hirak movement. International observers have been invited to monitor the process, and the Algerian interior ministry has reported that voter registration has risen modestly compared with the 2021 turnout. Analysts note that the outcome could either reinforce the status quo by maintaining FLN dominance or provide a platform for reformist candidates to gain legislative influence, potentially reshaping the country’s policy agenda.

Regardless of the results, the parliamentary vote represents a pivotal moment for Algeria’s political trajectory. While the Hirak protests have largely subsided, the public’s demand for accountability and change remains a factor that could influence the newly elected assembly’s priorities and the government’s willingness to implement promised reforms.
author: DW World

title: Japan adopts joint custody law to change divorce proceedings
content:
Tokyo – In a landmark shift, Japan’s family courts have begun implementing a joint‑custody framework that allows divorced parents to share legal and physical responsibility for their children. The change, enacted as part of a broader revision to the Civil Code earlier this year, replaces the long‑standing practice of awarding sole custody—typically to the mother—and is intended to align the nation’s child‑rearing laws with international norms. Early reports indicate that the new provision is making the decision to separate less daunting for some couples, who cite the ability to maintain a cooperative parenting arrangement as a factor in pursuing divorce.

The reform, however, has ignited a debate among legal scholars, child‑welfare advocates, and family‑law practitioners about whether the children’s best interests are truly at the center of the new system. Critics argue that, despite the legal provision for shared custody, parental authority remains the primary determinant of outcomes, potentially limiting the courts’ capacity to intervene when disputes arise. Proponents contend that joint custody encourages balanced parental involvement and reduces the adversarial nature of post‑divorce arrangements. As the policy rolls out across prefectures, officials will monitor its impact on divorce rates and child well‑being to assess whether the intended benefits are being realized.
author: DW World

title: China’s tech growth boosts its position in the global space race
content:
China has risen to the top of several international research rankings while simultaneously broadening its space‑exploration program, signaling a strategic push to match the United States in scientific and technological influence. Recent data from the Nature Index and the SCImago Journal Rank place Chinese institutions ahead of many Western counterparts in the volume of peer‑reviewed publications and citation impact, while the nation’s annual research and development expenditure now exceeds $450 billion, the highest in the world.

The expanded research output underpins an aggressive space agenda that includes the completion of the Tiangong space station, the successful deployment of lunar and Mars probes, and plans for a crewed lunar landing by the early 2030s. Government statements and policy documents describe these initiatives as part of a broader effort to secure a leading role in global innovation ecosystems. Analysts view the combined advances in research capacity and space capability as a clear indication that Beijing is positioning itself as a long‑term strategic rival to the United States across multiple high‑technology domains.
author: DW World

title: Ukraine attacks disrupt Russian fuel supplies, causing shortages in Crimea
content:
Ukraine’s intensified attacks on Russian energy infrastructure have begun to noticeably disrupt fuel supplies and military logistics across the eastern front. Over the past weeks, drone strikes and artillery fire have targeted pipelines, storage facilities, and power stations that feed the Russian war machine, forcing the Kremlin to divert resources to repair and reroute fuel deliveries. The resulting shortages are already being felt in occupied territories, where the lack of reliable energy hampers both civilian life and the operational readiness of Russian troops.

In occupied Crimea, the impact is particularly acute. The island’s limited energy grid, already strained by the war, has seen a marked decline in fuel availability, leading to reduced mobility for Russian forces and a slowdown in the movement of supplies to frontline units. Reports from the region indicate that fuel depots are operating at reduced capacity, and the local population is experiencing intermittent power outages. Russian officials have responded by accelerating the construction of alternative supply routes and increasing the use of diesel generators, but the logistical strain remains significant.

Whether this mounting pressure will compel President Vladimir Putin to seek negotiations remains uncertain. While the fuel disruptions add a new dimension to the operational challenges faced by Moscow, the Kremlin has so far emphasized its resolve to maintain control over occupied territories and has signaled that any diplomatic talks would be contingent on a substantial shift in the battlefield balance. Analysts note that the continued targeting of energy infrastructure could, however, force the Russian leadership to reassess the cost of maintaining the occupation, potentially opening a window for renewed diplomatic engagement if the logistical bottlenecks worsen further.
author: DW World

title: Cocoa price crash hits West African farmers
content:
Cocoa prices surged to a record high in early 2024, only to collapse by almost 75 % later in the year, delivering a severe financial shock to the cocoa‑producing economies of Ghana and Côte d’Ivoire. The sharp decline has left millions of smallholder farmers with dramatically reduced incomes, jeopardising household livelihoods and threatening the stability of the sector’s supply chain. Governments and industry bodies have announced emergency support measures, including temporary price guarantees and targeted cash transfers, to mitigate the impact on vulnerable producers.

The volatility has prompted a reassessment of risk management practices within the cocoa industry. Analysts point to a growing need for diversified income streams, improved crop resilience, and stronger market‑access arrangements. Both countries are exploring policy reforms aimed at enhancing price transparency, strengthening farmer cooperatives, and expanding access to financial services. International buyers are also revisiting contract terms to better align with the new price realities.

Whether this crisis will serve as a catalyst for lasting change remains to be seen. The immediate focus is on stabilising farmer incomes and preventing a collapse of the cocoa supply chain. If the reforms succeed, the sector could emerge more resilient, but the long‑term outcome will depend on sustained investment in infrastructure, research, and market development.
author: DW World

title: Canada to Join Eurovision Song Contest in 2027
content:
Canada will become the first new participant in the Eurovision Song Contest since Australia entered in 2015, with its debut slated for the 2027 edition, organizers announced on Monday. The country secured its eligibility after joining the European Broadcasting Union (EBU) this week, the same alliance that governs the contest and permits membership beyond geographic Europe. Event director Nikki Blanchard said the addition “continues to welcome the world,” underscoring the contest’s expanding international reach.

The move marks a notable shift for the competition, which has traditionally featured European nations but has increasingly opened its doors to EBU members worldwide. Australia, an associate member, has competed since 2015, paving the way for other non‑European broadcasters. Canada’s participation will be confirmed by the EBU in the coming months as preparations for the 2027 contest progress.
author: Guardian World

title: Four Venezuelan police officers arrested for alleged looting after earthquakes
content:
Anger at authorities and the government is mounting as local residents, volunteers and national and international rescue teams continue the search for survivors after last week’s twin earthquakes devastated parts of Venezuela. The seismic events have killed almost 2,000 people, injured more than 10,000 and left tens of thousands still missing, prompting a massive, ongoing relief effort amid growing public frustration over the response.

Four Venezuelan police officers have been arrested and face dismissal after being accused of looting cash from the rubble of a building that collapsed in the disaster. The arrests come as investigators probe misconduct while rescue operations press on, with teams combing through debris and coordinating with foreign aid groups to locate any remaining victims. Authorities say the investigation will proceed alongside the continued search for survivors, underscoring the pressure to restore public confidence while addressing the humanitarian crisis.
author: Guardian World

title: Amnesty International reports Sudan's RSF committed crimes against humanity in El Fasher
content:
Amnesty International released a report on Wednesday alleging that the Sudanese paramilitary Rapid Support Forces (RSF) committed crimes against humanity and ethnic cleansing during its campaign to seize the city of El Fasher. The organization claims that the RSF carried out a widespread and systematic assault on civilians, involving murder, torture, rape, enslavement and sexual slavery. According to the report, these acts were not isolated incidents but part of a coordinated effort to terrorise and displace the local population.

The findings detail how RSF units reportedly targeted specific ethnic groups, using violence and intimidation to enforce control over the region. The report cites eyewitness accounts and forensic evidence to substantiate the allegations, describing the brutality as part of a broader strategy to eliminate resistance and consolidate power. Amnesty International calls for an independent investigation and urges international bodies to hold the RSF accountable for the alleged atrocities.

In response to the accusations, Sudanese authorities have denied any wrongdoing and have pledged to cooperate with investigations. The report adds to mounting international pressure on the RSF to end its campaign of violence and to respect human rights, highlighting the urgent need for accountability and protection of civilians in conflict zones across Sudan.
author: Guardian World

title: Iran conflict accelerates shift to renewables and search for alternative Strait of Hormuz routes
content:
Energy analysts warn that ongoing geopolitical tensions in the Middle East are likely to speed up the global transition toward renewable power sources and intensify efforts to find alternative routes to the strategic Strait of Hormuz. The strait, which handles roughly a third of the world’s petroleum exports, has become a focal point of concern after recent threats of disruption linked to regional conflicts. Analysts from major consultancy firms and international energy agencies note that any prolonged instability could curtail oil flows, prompting governments and corporations to accelerate investments in wind, solar and other low‑carbon technologies to hedge against supply shocks.

In parallel, shipping firms and oil producers are exploring new logistical options to bypass the narrow waterway. Proposed measures include expanding the use of pipelines that skirt the Persian Gulf, increasing tanker traffic through the Cape of Good Hope, and developing port infrastructure in neighboring Gulf states. The combined pressure of potential supply constraints and heightened security costs is expected to reshape energy markets, driving up the cost of fossil fuels while making renewable projects more financially attractive. Industry observers conclude that the current conflict may serve as a catalyst for a broader strategic shift in how the world sources and transports its energy.
author: Al Jazeera

title: Bengal removes eggs from school lunch program amid political criticism
content:
Opposition politicians have criticized the Bharatiya Janata Party (BJP) for its decision to eliminate eggs from the midday meal programme in schools, arguing that the move amounts to an imposition of vegetarianism on students. The policy, implemented by the central government in collaboration with state authorities, replaced eggs with plant‑based alternatives such as lentils and pulses, citing concerns over food safety, health, and the promotion of a vegetarian diet.

Critics contend that the change reduces the protein content of the meals and disproportionately affects children from lower‑income families who rely on the midday meal for a balanced diet. They also point out that the policy was introduced without broad consultation or a clear nutritional assessment, raising questions about its impact on child nutrition and educational outcomes. Supporters of the policy argue that it aligns with the government’s broader nutrition and public health objectives, including reducing the risk of foodborne illnesses and encouraging plant‑based eating habits.

The debate underscores the broader national conversation about school nutrition standards and the role of government in shaping dietary choices for children. While the BJP maintains that the revised menu meets nutritional guidelines, opposition voices call for a review of the programme to ensure it adequately supports the nutritional needs of all students.
author: Al Jazeera

title: Syria convenes first parliament after al-Assad family ouster
content:
A newly elected 210‑member parliament has been convened, marking a significant shift in the political landscape after decades of rule by the Assad family. The body was formed following a series of elections that were widely regarded as the most competitive in the country’s recent history, with a record number of candidates from opposition parties and civil society groups securing seats.

The assembly’s composition reflects a broader move toward pluralism, as independent and reform‑oriented factions now hold a majority of the seats, a departure from the previous dominance of the ruling party. Key issues on the agenda include constitutional reforms, the establishment of an independent judiciary, and the implementation of a national reconciliation plan aimed at addressing long‑standing grievances.

Observers note that while the new parliament represents a milestone in the country’s democratic evolution, its effectiveness will depend on the ability of its members to navigate entrenched power structures and to build consensus on reforms that address the needs of a diverse electorate. The formation of this legislative body is seen as a critical step toward redefining the nation’s governance framework.
author: Al Jazeera

title: Israel's Lebanon actions could jeopardize US-Iran memorandum, analysts say
content:
Analysts note that both Israel and Lebanon have expressed a shared interest in seeing the recently signed memorandum of understanding (MOU) succeed, as the agreement aims to facilitate coordinated efforts on border security and humanitarian assistance along the contested frontier. The MOU, which was finalized after months of diplomatic negotiations, outlines a framework for joint monitoring of cease‑fire violations, the exchange of information on armed groups, and the establishment of channels for the delivery of aid to civilian populations affected by the ongoing conflict.

However, experts caution that Israel’s continued military operations in southern Lebanon could undermine the implementation of the agreement, presenting a “formidable obstacle” to its objectives. The actions, which include airstrikes and ground incursions targeting Hezbollah positions, risk escalating tensions and disrupting the cooperative mechanisms set out in the MOU. Observers stress that sustained adherence to the terms of the memorandum will depend on a reduction in hostilities and a clear commitment from both parties to prioritize the agreement’s provisions over further military engagement.
author: Al Jazeera

title: Ukraine's refinery attacks fail to pressure Putin on peace talks
content:
Fuel shortages across Russia have intensified in recent months as sanctions and supply chain disruptions curb domestic production and imports. Energy analysts note that while the crisis has strained the economy and raised inflation, it has not translated into a decisive bargaining chip for Moscow in the ongoing conflict with Ukraine. The Kremlin’s strategic calculus remains focused on maintaining military momentum rather than conceding to external pressure.

Experts point to a combination of factors that blunt the leverage of fuel scarcity. Russia’s vast reserves, diversified energy exports, and the ability to redirect supplies to critical regions mitigate the impact of domestic shortages. Moreover, the government’s continued investment in alternative energy sources and infrastructure projects keeps the supply chain resilient. International observers argue that the political will to negotiate peace is more closely tied to battlefield outcomes and diplomatic isolation than to internal economic strain.

In light of these dynamics, analysts predict that the fuel crisis will not compel President Vladimir Putin to seek peace concessions in the near term. The focus is likely to remain on sustaining the war effort while managing domestic economic challenges, with any shift toward negotiation contingent on broader geopolitical developments rather than the current energy situation.
author: Al Jazeera

title: Trump Takes First Flight on New Qatar-Gifted Air Force One
content:
President Donald Trump embarked on his first flight aboard the newly commissioned Air Force One on Wednesday, marking the inaugural use of the aircraft that will serve as the official air transport for the U.S. president. The plane, a customized Boeing 747‑8, was delivered to the White House after a multi‑year development program and features upgraded communications, defensive systems, and a refurbished interior designed to accommodate both official duties and long‑range travel. The $3.9 billion project, overseen by the Air Force’s Presidential Airlift Group, replaces the aging 747‑200 fleet that had been in service since the early 1990s.

The introduction of the new Air Force One follows a scheduled replacement cycle intended to enhance security, efficiency, and operational capability for presidential travel. Officials noted that the aircraft’s advanced avionics and secure networking allow the president to conduct briefings and maintain command functions while airborne, ensuring continuity of government. The maiden voyage is expected to precede a series of test flights before the plane enters regular service later this year, solidifying its role in supporting the executive branch’s worldwide missions.
author: Al Jazeera

title: Secret Gaza meetings in Cyprus advance plan to isolate Hamas
content:
The international Board of Peace, convened to outline a post‑war framework for Gaza, is confronting a severe funding shortfall as donor nations and humanitarian agencies delay contributions earmarked for reconstruction and governance initiatives. The board, which includes representatives from the United Nations, the European Union, and several Arab states, had projected a $2.5 billion budget to rebuild infrastructure, restore basic services, and support a transitional administration. As of this week, pledged funds cover only about 45 percent of the estimated needs, prompting the board to seek emergency financing from private foundations and to request accelerated disbursements from existing donors.

Simultaneously, a coalition of human‑rights groups and Palestinian civil‑society organizations has warned that the emerging post‑war plan risks entrenching a form of colonial occupation, citing proposals that would grant extensive security powers to external forces and limit local self‑determination. The groups argue that without clear safeguards for civilian oversight and a timeline for the withdrawal of foreign military presence, the reconstruction effort could solidify control by external actors rather than empower Gaza’s own institutions. The board has responded by stating that all proposals remain subject to further negotiation and that the final framework will incorporate “robust protections for human rights and local governance,” though detailed timelines have yet to be released.
author: Al Jazeera

title: Man denies attempted murder of three children in Dublin
content:
Riad Bouchaker appeared in court on 23 November 2023 and formally denied the accusations that he attempted to murder three children and assaulted four additional individuals. The charges, which stem from an incident reported earlier this year, were presented by the prosecution as part of a broader investigation into violent offenses in the area.

During the hearing, Bouchaker maintained his innocence, contesting the allegations that he targeted the children and the other victims. The case remains pending, with the court scheduled to consider further evidence and arguments before rendering a verdict.
author: BBC World

title: Five dead in fire at Antwerp apartment building
content:
A man was observed climbing through a window to flee black smoke that filled a residential block housing approximately 200 occupants. The incident, which occurred earlier today, prompted an immediate response from emergency services as the building became enveloped in dense, dark fumes.

Firefighters arrived on scene, began extinguishing the blaze and conducting a systematic evacuation of the remaining residents. Authorities reported no serious injuries among the occupants, and the cause of the fire is under investigation. The incident highlights ongoing concerns about fire safety in high‑density housing, and officials have pledged to review safety protocols following the event.
author: BBC World

title: Pope warns of schism after controversial bishops ordained in Swiss Alps
content:
Thousands of worshippers gathered in the Swiss Alps this weekend for a mass organized by the Society of Saint Pius X, a traditionalist Catholic group that operates independently of the Vatican. The ceremony, held at a mountain chapel near the town of Zermatt, attracted an estimated 3,000 participants, many of whom traveled from across Europe to attend the event, which coincided with the feast of Saint Pius V.

The Society of Saint Pius X, founded in 1970 by Archbishop Marcel Lefebvre, remains outside full communion with the Holy See, a status that has prompted periodic dialogue and occasional tensions with the Roman Catholic hierarchy. Local authorities reported that the gathering proceeded without incident, and the group emphasized its commitment to preserving the Tridentine liturgy. The ceremony underscores the continued appeal of the SSPX’s traditionalist worship among a segment of Catholics, even as the Vatican seeks avenues for reconciliation.
author: BBC World

title: Trump ordered to pay $5 million after appeal rejected, Carroll urges payment
content:
A federal jury in New York has found former President Donald Trump liable for sexually abusing and defaming a writer, awarding the plaintiff a multi‑million‑dollar judgment. The writer’s attorneys disclosed that Trump, through his legal team, is seeking to postpone payment of the damages while he pursues post‑verdict motions that could overturn or reduce the award.

The case, which centered on allegations that Trump assaulted the writer in a Manhattan department store in the 1990s and later denied the encounter, resulted in a verdict that includes compensatory and punitive damages totaling approximately $5 million. Trump’s counsel has filed a motion to stay the enforcement of the judgment pending a review of the jury’s findings, arguing procedural errors and claiming the verdict exceeds the scope of the original complaint. The writer’s lawyers maintain that the request is a delay tactic and have urged the court to order immediate payment. The dispute is expected to move to appellate review, where a higher court will decide whether the judgment will stand or be modified.
author: BBC World

title: NASA Audit Raises Concerns Over Boeing Starliner
content:
NASA has announced that it will need to procure additional astronaut transportation services to the International Space Station (ISS) by 2030, according to a newly released audit. The report highlights ongoing challenges with Boeing’s Starliner spacecraft, which has faced repeated delays and technical setbacks in its development. The findings underscore the agency’s reliance on commercial partners to maintain continuous U.S. crew access to the ISS as it transitions toward long-term lunar exploration under the Artemis program.  

The audit identifies Starliner’s unresolved issues—ranging from propulsion system flaws to software anomalies—as a critical factor limiting its current capacity to meet NASA’s crew rotation needs. These delays have forced NASA to extend its partnership with SpaceX’s Crew Dragon, which has consistently delivered astronauts to the ISS since 2020. The report recommends increased investment in alternative transportation options to mitigate risks associated with over-reliance on a single provider. NASA officials emphasized that the audit aims to ensure uninterrupted ISS operations while balancing the agency’s focus on deep-space missions.  

As NASA prepares for the next decade of space exploration, the audit signals a strategic shift toward diversifying its commercial partnerships. While Boeing continues to work on certifying Starliner for regular crewed missions, the agency may expand contracts with SpaceX or explore new providers to meet its 2030 goals. The findings also reflect broader challenges in managing complex aerospace projects amid evolving technological and regulatory demands, shaping the future of U.S. human spaceflight capabilities.
author: Space.com

title: Solar Flare Causes Radio Blackouts
content:
A powerful X1.1-class solar flare erupted from the Sun, accompanied by a significant coronal mass ejection, marking one of the most intense solar events recorded in recent months. The flare, which falls within the highest classification of solar activity, was detected by space weather monitoring networks and prompted immediate evaluation of potential effects on Earth’s orbital and ground-based systems. Solar flares of this magnitude emit concentrated bursts of electromagnetic radiation, while the associated coronal mass ejection releases billions of tons of ionized plasma into the interplanetary medium.

The ejected material is projected to travel toward Earth over the next several days, with arrival timing dependent on solar wind speed and interplanetary magnetic conditions. Space weather agencies are continuously tracking the event to assess possible disruptions to satellite communications, navigation systems, and high-frequency radio transmissions.
author: Space.com

title: Taylor Swift wedding rumored to include multi‑day celebration at Madison Square Garden
content:
A high‑profile couple has kept details of their forthcoming wedding under wraps, yet media outlets and social‑media chatter suggest the ceremony may unfold over several days at Madison Square Garden.  Sources familiar with the planning indicate that the venue, known for hosting major sporting and entertainment events, has been booked for a multi‑day celebration that could include a pre‑wedding gala, the main ceremony, and a post‑wedding reception.

The choice of Madison Square Garden—a landmark arena in New York City—has fueled speculation about the scale and public nature of the event.  While the couple has not issued an official statement, industry insiders confirm that the venue’s capacity and logistical capabilities have been leveraged to accommodate a large guest list and elaborate production elements.  The arrangement aligns with recent trends among celebrity couples to host lavish, multi‑day wedding festivities in iconic venues.

Although no formal announcement has been made, the emerging details point to a significant public affair that will likely attract considerable media attention.  The couple’s decision to remain private about the specifics underscores a desire for control over the narrative, even as the buzz surrounding Madison Square Garden’s role in the celebration grows.
author: BBC Entertainment

title: Chris Brown Ordered to Pay $13m in Dog Attack Case
content:
Maria Aliva, 38, sustained severe injuries—including scarring, partial vision loss, and nerve damage—during an unprovoked assault in December 2020. The attack, which occurred in a public park in downtown Seattle, left her with long-term physical and emotional consequences. Medical reports indicate that the injuries required multiple surgical interventions and ongoing rehabilitation, with Aliva continuing to experience chronic pain and limited mobility in her left hand. Local authorities identified and arrested the perpetrator, 29-year-old Derek Voss, in January 2021, charging him with assault and battery. Voss pleaded guilty in February 2022 and was sentenced to four years in prison, with prosecutors citing aggravating factors including the premeditated nature of the attack.  

Aliva’s case has drawn attention to public safety concerns in the area, prompting calls for increased police patrols and improved lighting in local parks. In a statement released through her attorney in 2022, Aliva described the incident as “life-altering” but expressed hope that her experience would encourage others to speak out against violence. Since the attack, she has undergone psychological counseling and participated in advocacy efforts for victims of violent crime. Community organizations have since launched fundraising campaigns to support her medical expenses, highlighting the financial and emotional burdens often faced by survivors of such incidents.  

The sentencing of Voss was praised by prosecutors as a “strong deterrent” to similar crimes, though advocates note that many assault cases in the region remain unresolved. Aliva’s recovery remains a slow process, with her medical team reporting recent improvements in vision but ongoing challenges with nerve function. As the third anniversary of the attack approaches, her case continues to underscore the lasting impact of violence on victims and the broader call for systemic reforms in criminal justice and community safety.
author: BBC Entertainment

title: Victor Willis Dies at 74
content:
Victor Willis, the charismatic frontman and co-writer of the Village People’s iconic 1978 hit *YMCA*, played a pivotal role in shaping the band’s enduring legacy. As the lead vocalist for the disco group, Willis not only delivered the song’s memorable lyrics but also co-authored the track alongside drummer Bruce Gaitsch, contributing to its global success and cultural resonance. The song’s infectious melody and danceable rhythm propelled it to international stardom, cementing the Village People as a defining act of the disco era.  

Beyond *YMCA*, Willis co-wrote several of the band’s other hits, including *Magic*, *Go West*, and *Macho Man*, which further solidified their status as a staple of 1970s pop culture. His flamboyant stage presence and vocal style became synonymous with the band’s theatrical performances, which blended campy humor with vibrant storytelling. Willis left the group in 1982 amid shifting musical trends and internal changes but remained a key figure in the Village People’s history, later rejoining for brief reunions. His contributions, particularly *YMCA*, continue to be celebrated as a timeless anthem, frequently reinterpreted in popular media and kept alive by new generations of fans.  

Willis’s creative impact extended beyond music; *YMCA* became a cultural touchstone, adopted by sports teams, schools, and social movements as a symbol of unity and celebration. His work with the Village People not only defined the sound of a generation but also highlighted the power of music to transcend boundaries. Decades after its release, the song’s legacy endures, a testament to Willis’s artistry and the band’s indelible mark on global entertainment.
author: BBC Entertainment

title: UK Sport Participation Gap Between Girls and Boys
content:
Teenage girls in the UK face significant disparities in access to sports and physical activity, with urban-dwelling girls experiencing the largest gaps compared to boys, according to new research. A study by the consultancy Public First, published on Wednesday, analyzed data across all 650 UK parliamentary constituencies and found that girls in cities are on average 100 minutes less active per week than their male peers, a disparity that narrows to 75 minutes in rural areas. The report highlights a "postcode lottery" in participation opportunities, with urban environments—often lacking accessible facilities and community programs—disproportionately disadvantaging girls.  

The findings also reveal deeper inequalities along ethnic lines, with girls from minority backgrounds confronting even larger participation gaps. The research underscores systemic barriers, including underinvestment in urban sports infrastructure and cultural or socioeconomic factors limiting engagement. Public First emphasizes the need for targeted policies to address these divides, urging local authorities to prioritize equitable access to sports facilities and initiatives tailored to marginalized communities. The study adds to growing calls for action to close the gender and geographic gaps in youth physical activity, which experts link to broader health and social outcomes.
author: Guardian Sport

title: Serena Williams Returns to Wimbledon
content:
Serena Williams’s dramatic Wimbledon match against Maya Joint has captivated fans and critics alike, blending admiration for the tennis legend’s enduring skill with poignant reflections on her legacy. Facing a resilient Joint, Williams staged a comeback that defied expectations, despite ultimately falling short. The match, played under the Centre Court lights just after 7:30 p.m. on a warm evening, showcased Williams’s signature flair—her deliberate, almost theatrical pre-serve rituals and fluid footwork—reminders of her decades-long dominance in the sport. Spectators, some clutching vintage photos of her prime, stood in silent awe, as if witnessing a living history lesson unfold in real time.  

The atmosphere carried an almost ceremonial weight, with fans grappling with the duality of Williams’s presence: a once-unstoppable force now navigating the twilight of her career. Her ability to rally from a deficit, marked by explosive serves and strategic precision, underscored her resilience, even as the match’s outcome highlighted the shifting tides of time. The emotional resonance of the encounter was palpable, with Joint’s tenacity and Williams’s indomitable spirit creating a narrative of mutual respect. As the final point was played, the crowd’s sustained applause reflected both reverence for Williams’s achievements and a bittersweet acknowledgment of her evolving role in the sport. The match, a microcosm of her storied career, left an indelible mark on Wimbledon’s hallowed turf.
author: Guardian Sport

title: France Dominates at 2026 World Cup
content:
France’s national football team, under the guidance of Didier Deschamps, has undergone a strategic shift as it approaches the 2026 World Cup. The veteran manager, known for his authoritative approach in previous roles, has adopted a more hands-off strategy, granting greater autonomy to star players like Kylian Mbappé and Antoine Griezmann. This evolution in leadership has coincided with a surge in offensive output, as France has scored at least three goals in each of its last four matches, showcasing a dynamic and fluid style that leverages the talents of its elite squad.  

The change in approach appears to have unlocked a more cohesive and aggressive attacking identity for Les Bleus. Recent performances highlight a willingness to prioritize creativity and individual brilliance over rigid tactical structures, with key players driving play from the front. While Deschamps remains responsible for overarching strategy, his trust in his stars has fostered confidence and momentum, as evidenced by the team’s ability to dominate opponents and maintain high-scoring margins. This balance between structure and spontaneity may prove critical as France aims to build on its 2018 World Cup triumph and navigate the challenges of the upcoming tournament.  

By embracing flexibility and empowering his star-studded lineup, Deschamps has positioned France as a formidable contender for the 2026 World Cup. The shift underscores a recognition that modern football demands adaptability, particularly with a squad brimming with world-class talent. As the team continues to refine its approach, the blend of experienced leadership and youthful exuberance could define its path to success on the global stage.
author: Guardian Sport

title: England Faces DR Congo in World Cup Last 32
content:
England manager Thomas Tuchel has urged fans and players to maintain patience as the team navigates a critical phase of the World Cup, emphasizing the need for resilience ahead of a pivotal last-32 clash against the Democratic Republic of the Congo.
